pages.description: The tool catalog — users land here first.

findings.observation: The entire catalog content area behind the role-selection modal is completely black and empty — no tool cards, categories, or skeleton content is visible at any point during onboarding.
findings.impact: Users have no preview of what Forge contains before committing to a role, reducing motivation to engage with the selection and creating a sense of an unfinished product.
findings.fix: Render a blurred or dimmed version of the catalog grid behind the modal so users can see the value they're about to personalize, or add a short value-prop line listing example tool categories.

findings.observation: There is no 'Skip' or 'Continue without selecting' option on the role-selection modal. The only way to proceed appears to be choosing one of the 8 role buttons, but none of them has a highlighted or primary-action state to guide the user.
findings.impact: Users who don't identify with any listed role (e.g., a manager, exec, or cross-functional hire) are blocked from accessing the catalog entirely, causing drop-off.
findings.fix: Add a low-emphasis 'Skip for now' text link below the button grid, and consider giving the buttons a hover/focus state with a visible border or background tint to reinforce they are interactive.

findings.observation: Role labels use internal abbreviations: 'AE', 'SDR', 'CS', 'Eng'. These are reasonable for a sales-org tool but 'Eng' is inconsistently abbreviated while 'RevOps', 'Product', and 'Recruiter' are spelled out in full.
findings.impact: New employees or cross-functional users unfamiliar with company shorthand may not know which role to select, leading to incorrect personalization.
findings.fix: Either abbreviate consistently or spell out all role names fully (e.g., 'Account Executive', 'Sales Dev Rep', 'Customer Success', 'Engineering'). A tooltip or sub-label on hover could also clarify.

findings.observation: All 8 role buttons share identical dark background (#2a2a2a approx.) with white text and no visible focus ring, border, or differentiation in their resting state.
findings.impact: Keyboard-only and screen-reader users cannot distinguish focus position between buttons, failing WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.7 (Focus Visible).
findings.fix: Add a 2px solid focus ring in a high-contrast accent color (e.g., white or brand blue) on :focus-visible for all buttons. Consider a subtle border on resting state to reinforce interactivity.

findings.observation: The top nav shows 'Home', 'Skills', 'My Forge', and '+ Publish' links while the modal is open, meaning users can navigate away from onboarding before completing role selection.
findings.impact: Users may click a nav item, bypass role selection entirely, and receive an un-personalized catalog experience without realizing it.
findings.fix: Disable or visually mute nav links while the first-run modal is active, or save the modal state so it re-appears if dismissed without a selection.

findings.observation: The crossed-hammer icon above 'Welcome to Forge' is small (~24px) and low-contrast against the dark modal background, providing little brand reinforcement at the top of the first impression screen.
findings.impact: The onboarding moment feels generic and low-energy; the brand mark doesn't register before users read the headline.
findings.fix: Increase the icon to ~40px, add a subtle colored glow or tinted background pill, or replace with a wordmark lockup to strengthen brand recall on first contact.

findings.observation: On the 1440px desktop layout, the modal is centered in a ~460px wide card and surrounded by vast empty black space on all sides, leaving roughly 80% of the viewport unused.
findings.impact: The page feels sparse and unfinished for a first-time user on a large monitor, wasting an opportunity to show catalog previews or feature highlights alongside the modal.
findings.fix: Either widen the modal to ~600px with a two-column role grid, or add flanking content (e.g., featured tool cards, a 'trending this week' strip) that becomes interactive after role selection.

findings.observation: On 375px, the modal card has visible top/bottom margin but the bottom edge of the card is very close to the viewport bottom with little breathing room, and the content below ('What's your role...') wraps to two lines making it feel cramped.
findings.impact: The modal feels tight on smaller devices, and any system UI (home indicator bar on iOS) may visually overlap the bottom of the card.
findings.fix: Add at least 24px safe-area-inset padding to the modal's bottom edge and reduce the font size of the subtitle to 14px to prevent two-line wrap within the card width.

findings.observation: The breadcrumb area shows 'Home > Skills > My Forge > + Publish' which mirrors the top nav items rather than representing a true hierarchical path on the catalog page.
findings.impact: Users following the breadcrumb expect it to show location context (e.g., 'Home > Catalog'), not a repeat of the global nav, causing confusion about where they are.
findings.fix: Replace the nav-mirroring breadcrumb with a true location breadcrumb ('Home / Catalog') or remove it entirely on the root catalog page where it adds no navigational value.

strengths: The role-personalization prompt on first visit is a strong UX pattern — surfacing it immediately as a modal before showing the catalog prevents an overwhelming un-filtered experience.
The 2×4 button grid for role selection is clean, evenly weighted, and scannable with no visual noise competing for attention inside the modal.
pages.summary: The catalog's first-run onboarding modal has a sound personalization concept but is undermined by a lack of skip affordance, inaccessible focus states, ambiguous role abbreviations, and a completely empty backdrop that makes the product feel unfinished before the user even begins.

pages.description: A prompt-tool detail page with run form.

findings.observation: The onboarding role-selection modal overlays a completely black, empty page. There is no underlying content visible — the entire product surface behind the modal is blank.
findings.impact: Users have no context for what Forge is or what they are personalizing before committing to a role, reducing confidence and increasing drop-off on the first interaction.
findings.fix: Show a blurred or dimmed preview of the actual tool grid behind the modal so users understand what they are about to see, providing immediate product value context.

findings.observation: There is no way to dismiss or skip the role-selection modal — no 'Skip', 'Close', or 'Maybe later' option is present anywhere in the overlay.
findings.impact: Users who don't identify with any listed role, or who simply want to explore first, are hard-blocked and cannot proceed without making an arbitrary selection.
findings.fix: Add a 'Skip for now' text link below the role grid, or a close (×) icon in the top-right corner of the modal.

findings.observation: The role labels are bare abbreviations: 'AE', 'SDR', 'CS', 'RevOps', 'Eng'. 'AE', 'SDR', and 'CS' are jargon that may not be universally understood even inside the target company.
findings.impact: Users unfamiliar with these internal acronyms cannot make an informed selection, leading to mis-categorization and a poorly personalized experience.
findings.fix: Expand abbreviations with a short sub-label, e.g. 'AE — Account Executive', 'SDR — Sales Dev Rep', 'CS — Customer Success'.

findings.observation: All eight role buttons have identical visual weight — same size, same dark fill, no hover/focus state visible in the static screenshot — making none stand out as a suggested or common choice.
findings.impact: Users spend more cognitive effort scanning all options equally rather than being guided toward the most common role selections.
findings.fix: Apply a subtle highlight or badge (e.g. 'Most common') to the top 1–2 roles, and ensure hover and focus states are visually distinct with a border or background shift.

findings.observation: The top navigation bar (Home, Skills, My Forge, + Publish) and the search bar are fully visible and interactive behind the modal without any overlay scrim blocking them.
findings.impact: Users can interact with nav items while the modal is open, potentially bypassing onboarding entirely in an unintended way, causing an incomplete personalization state.
findings.fix: Add a semi-transparent dark scrim (e.g. rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) behind the modal that covers the nav and search bar to enforce modal focus, while still allowing dismissal via the scrim click if a skip action is intentional.

findings.observation: The role buttons use a dark background (#2a2a2a approx.) with white text but no visible focus ring. Keyboard navigation and screen-reader tab order through the 2-column grid is not indicated.
findings.impact: Keyboard-only and assistive technology users may not be able to reliably navigate or activate role options, failing WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.7.
findings.fix: Add a high-contrast focus ring (e.g. 2px solid #FFFFFF or a brand accent color) on all interactive elements, and ensure the modal traps focus on open.

findings.observation: The hammer-and-pick icon above 'Welcome to Forge' is small (~24px) and low contrast against the dark modal background, making it feel decorative rather than purposeful.
findings.impact: The icon adds no navigational or informational value at its current size and contrast, contributing to visual noise without reinforcing brand identity.
findings.fix: Either increase the icon size and add a subtle glow or tinted background chip to make it a meaningful brand moment, or remove it to tighten the modal's vertical rhythm.

findings.observation: On mobile (375px), the modal does not extend to full-screen height — it floats with visible black space above and below — and the bottom of the modal is slightly cropped, hinting at scroll.
findings.impact: The floating modal with empty space around it feels unanchored on a small screen, and any cropping may cause users to miss the last row of options without realizing they need to scroll.
findings.fix: On mobile, convert the modal to a bottom sheet that extends to at least 90% viewport height, or ensure all content fits without scroll. If scroll is needed, add a visible scroll indicator.

strengths: The 2-column grid layout for role selection is spatially efficient and scannable, presenting all options without requiring scroll on desktop.
The modal's dark card on dark background maintains the product's consistent dark-theme aesthetic without jarring contrast breaks.
pages.summary: The role-selection onboarding modal is functional but creates a hard block on an empty page with no skip path, unexplained jargon abbreviations, and insufficient focus/accessibility states that together risk high first-visit drop-off.

pages.description: Tool submission — first step asks which format.

findings.observation: The modal heading says 'Welcome to Forge' but the user is already inside Forge (breadcrumb shows Home > Skills > My Forge > Publish). This is a role-selection step, not an onboarding welcome.
findings.impact: Users are confused about whether they've landed somewhere new or triggered a dialog mid-flow; 'Welcome' implies first-time entry and undermines trust in the navigation context.
findings.fix: Change heading to 'Choose your role' or 'Which role best describes you?' and subtitle to 'We'll surface the most relevant tools for you.' Remove 'Welcome to Forge' entirely.

findings.observation: There is no visible way to dismiss the modal (no X button, no backdrop click affordance, no Skip or Cancel option). The user is forced to select a role before proceeding.
findings.impact: Users who arrived via a direct link or who simply want to browse all tools without committing to a role are completely blocked. It also breaks back-navigation expectations.
findings.fix: Add a clearly labeled 'Skip' or 'Browse all' text link at the bottom of the modal, and an X icon in the top-right corner. Optionally allow backdrop click to dismiss.

findings.observation: All eight role buttons (AE, SDR, RevOps, CS, Product, Eng, Recruiter, Other) look identical in style with no hover states visible in the static screenshots and no indication of which, if any, is currently selected or pre-selected.
findings.impact: Users cannot tell if they have already chosen a role in a previous session, or whether selecting a role is a permanent preference vs. a one-time filter. There is no selected state to confirm their action before proceeding.
findings.fix: Add a visible selected state (e.g., highlighted border, filled background, checkmark) on tap/click. If a role was previously saved, pre-select it. Include a 'Confirm' or 'Continue' button that activates after a selection is made.

findings.observation: The two-column grid of role options has no descriptions or tooltips explaining what each role abbreviation (AE, SDR, CS) means for users who are not familiar with the internal naming convention.
findings.impact: New employees or cross-functional users (e.g., someone in finance or legal) cannot self-identify and may select the wrong role or 'Other' by default, leading to irrelevant tool recommendations.
findings.fix: Add a short descriptor beneath each abbreviation (e.g., 'AE — Account Executive') or provide a tooltip on hover/long-press. Alternatively, spell out the full role name as the primary label.

findings.observation: The modal appears to have no visible focus ring or keyboard-navigable structure indicated in the screenshots. The dark button borders on a near-black background (#1a1a1a buttons on #242424 modal) have very low contrast.
findings.impact: Keyboard-only and low-vision users may be unable to identify focusable elements or read the button labels, potentially failing WCAG 1.4.3 minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1.
findings.fix: Increase button border or background contrast so labels meet at least 4.5:1 against the modal background. Add a visible focus ring style (e.g., 2px white or brand-color outline) for keyboard navigation.

findings.observation: The crossed-hammer icon at the top of the modal is the Forge logo/icon but serves no functional purpose here and competes visually with the heading.
findings.impact: Minor cognitive noise; the icon does not reinforce the action being requested (role selection) and adds vertical height to the modal without utility.
findings.fix: Remove the icon from this step or replace it with a more contextually relevant icon (e.g., a person/role icon) that reinforces the role-selection action.

findings.observation: On mobile (375px), the modal floats with visible page content (search bar, breadcrumb) showing behind it but with no visible overlay/scrim darkening the background.
findings.impact: Without a scrim, the modal does not clearly read as blocking the underlying page, potentially confusing users about whether the background content is still interactive.
findings.fix: Apply a semi-transparent dark overlay (e.g., rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) behind the modal on mobile to visually isolate it and communicate that background interaction is disabled.

strengths: The two-column grid layout scales cleanly from desktop to mobile with consistent button sizing and spacing, requiring no horizontal scrolling.
The dark modal on dark background maintains the product's visual identity without introducing jarring color shifts during the role-selection interruption.
pages.summary: The role-selection modal blocks the publish flow with no escape path, uses ambiguous abbreviations without explanation, and lacks selected-state feedback — all of which create friction and confusion at a critical first step.

findings.observation: The role-selection buttons (AE, SDR, RevOps, CS, Product, Eng, Recruiter, Other) have no selected/active state visible. There is no confirmation action (e.g., a 'Continue' button) after choosing a role, leaving users uncertain whether clicking a button does anything or how to proceed.
findings.impact: Users may click a role and receive no feedback, causing confusion about whether their selection was registered or what happens next.
findings.fix: Add a visually distinct selected state (e.g., highlighted border or filled background) to the chosen role button, and introduce a 'Continue' CTA that activates after a selection is made, making the two-step flow explicit.

findings.observation: The role buttons use very low-contrast dark text on a dark button background (dark gray on near-black). There is no visible focus ring or keyboard-navigation indicator on any interactive element in the modal.
findings.impact: Users with low vision or those navigating via keyboard cannot reliably read button labels or determine which element is focused, likely failing WCAG 2.1 AA contrast and focus-visibility requirements.
findings.fix: Increase button label contrast to at least 4.5:1 against the button background, and apply a clearly visible focus ring (e.g., 2px offset outline in a brand accent color) to all interactive elements.

findings.observation: The role abbreviations 'AE', 'SDR', 'CS', and 'Eng' are industry jargon that may not be universally understood, especially for users who are new to the platform or come from non-sales backgrounds.
findings.impact: Users unfamiliar with these acronyms cannot confidently select the most relevant role, potentially skewing their app recommendations and reducing onboarding quality.
findings.fix: Expand abbreviations with a short descriptor beneath each label (e.g., 'AE — Account Executive', 'SDR — Sales Development Rep', 'CS — Customer Success', 'Eng — Engineering') or use full role names within the button.

findings.observation: The top navigation bar shows 'Home', 'Skills', 'My Forge', and '+ Publish' links while the role-selection modal is open, but these links appear to remain interactive behind the modal, which does not use a true overlay/backdrop.
findings.impact: Users could navigate away from the onboarding modal accidentally before completing role selection, bypassing a step the product clearly intends them to complete.
findings.fix: Apply a semi-transparent dark backdrop behind the modal and disable pointer events on the navigation links while the modal is active, or use a dedicated full-screen onboarding route instead of a floating modal.

findings.observation: The search bar at the top of the page is fully visible and accessible while the role-selection modal is open, implying users can search for tools before their role context has been set.
findings.impact: This undermines the stated purpose of the modal ('We'll show you the most relevant apps first'), since a user can skip role selection entirely by using search, making the personalization step feel optional and inconsistent.
findings.fix: Disable or visually dim the search bar until the role-selection modal is dismissed, or move the role-selection step to a true first-run experience before the main UI is rendered.

findings.observation: On the 1440px desktop viewport, the modal is centered in a very large black void with no surrounding content, making roughly 70% of the screen completely unused black space.
findings.impact: The screen feels unfinished and wastes prime real estate; users may perceive the page as broken or still loading rather than intentionally focused.
findings.fix: Either constrain the desktop background with a subtle texture, blurred app-grid preview, or brand illustration, or expand the modal to a more proportionate max-width (e.g., 600–700px) with more breathing room inside.

findings.observation: The hammer-and-pick icon above 'Welcome to Forge' is small (roughly 24px) and low contrast against the dark modal background, making it decorative at best and invisible at worst.
findings.impact: The icon adds no scannable value at its current size and contrast, missing an opportunity to reinforce brand identity or communicate the tool-building theme.
findings.fix: Increase the icon size to 40–48px and ensure its fill color has sufficient contrast against the modal background, or replace it with a branded lockup that carries more visual weight.

findings.observation: On the 375px viewport, the modal card clips near the bottom of the visible area with minimal bottom margin, and the page background is entirely black with no scroll affordance visible.
findings.impact: On shorter devices (e.g., iPhone SE at 667px or landscape mode), the bottom role buttons ('Recruiter', 'Other') may be partially obscured, requiring unexpected scrolling that users may not discover.
findings.fix: Add sufficient vertical padding inside the modal and ensure the card is vertically centered within the viewport with a max-height and internal scroll if needed, tested at 568px viewport height.

findings.observation: The button grid uses a 2-column layout with 4 rows, placing 'Recruiter' and 'Other' in the final row. 'Other' is a catch-all but is given equal visual weight and size as specific role titles.
findings.impact: Users may feel uncertain whether 'Other' is a valid path or a fallback, and it may be selected too readily if users don't identify with any listed role.
findings.fix: Visually differentiate 'Other' as a secondary option — for example, render it as a text link below the button grid or use a lighter button style — to signal it is a fallback rather than a primary role.

strengths: The modal's focused single-question design ('What's your role?') effectively reduces cognitive load for first-time users by presenting one clear decision before revealing the full marketplace.
The consistent 2-column button grid is clean and spatially balanced across both desktop and mobile viewports, requiring minimal responsive adjustment.
pages.summary: The Forge onboarding modal has a clear, focused intent but is undermined by missing selected-state feedback, no explicit confirmation action, accessibility contrast failures, and insufficient isolation from the surrounding navigable UI, all of which risk users skipping or misunderstanding the personalization step.

findings.observation: The role-selection buttons (AE, SDR, RevOps, CS, Product, Eng, Recruiter, Other) have no selected/active state visible. There is no primary CTA to confirm the selection and proceed, nor any indication of what happens after a role is chosen.
findings.impact: Users cannot tell if tapping a role selects it or immediately navigates away, and there is no clear path forward after choosing, causing confusion and potential drop-off at the very first interaction.
findings.fix: Add a visible selected state (e.g. highlighted border or filled background) to the chosen button and introduce a 'Continue' or 'Get Started' CTA that activates once a role is picked. Alternatively, auto-advance with a brief visual confirmation so the interaction model is unambiguous.

findings.observation: The breadcrumb trail shows 'Home > Skills > My Forge > + Publish' while a modal onboarding overlay is displayed, implying the user is simultaneously on a workflow-builder page and being asked their role for the first time.
findings.impact: The context mismatch between the breadcrumb state and the onboarding modal creates disorientation — it is unclear whether this is a first-run experience or a mid-session interrupt, eroding trust in the navigation system.
findings.fix: Block or hide the breadcrumb (and the search bar behind it) while the onboarding modal is active, or trigger the role-selection step before the user reaches any page-level content so the UI state is coherent.

findings.observation: The role labels use insider abbreviations: 'AE' (Account Executive), 'SDR' (Sales Development Rep), 'CS' (Customer Success), 'Eng' (Engineering). No tooltips or expanded labels are provided.
findings.impact: New hires, contractors, or cross-functional users unfamiliar with internal role naming conventions may select the wrong persona, causing them to see irrelevant app recommendations throughout their session.
findings.fix: Expand abbreviations inline or on hover/long-press (e.g. 'AE – Account Executive') so all users can self-identify correctly without tribal knowledge.

findings.observation: The role buttons appear to be rendered as plain buttons on a very dark background (#1a1a1a-equivalent) with medium-grey text. Contrast between button label text and button background is visually borderline and likely fails WCAG AA 4.5:1 for normal-sized text.
findings.impact: Users with low vision or in suboptimal lighting conditions may struggle to read the role labels, reducing inclusivity and potentially violating accessibility compliance requirements.
findings.fix: Increase button label text to white (#FFFFFF) or near-white and ensure button backgrounds are sufficiently distinct from the modal background, verifying contrast ratios meet WCAG AA minimums before shipping.

findings.observation: On desktop, the modal is a small island (roughly 460px wide) centered in a completely black, empty 1440px canvas. The background page content — the workflow composer — is entirely invisible behind the solid black fill.
findings.impact: The full-black backdrop makes the product feel unfinished and wastes the opportunity to give users contextual anticipation of what they are about to build, which could motivate role selection.
findings.fix: Replace the solid black overlay with a semi-transparent dark scrim (e.g. rgba(0,0,0,0.7)) over a blurred or dimmed version of the actual workflow canvas, so users understand the interface they are personalising.

findings.observation: The hammer-and-pick icon above 'Welcome to Forge' is the same white color and similar weight as the heading, causing the icon and title to compete rather than the icon serving as a subordinate decorative element.
findings.impact: The eye is not clearly directed to the heading first; the pairing reads as two equal-weight elements, slightly slowing comprehension of the screen's purpose.
findings.fix: Reduce the icon's opacity to ~50-60% or decrease its size relative to the heading so it functions as supportive branding rather than a co-headline.

findings.observation: On mobile (375px), the modal card begins very close to the top of the viewport (approximately 25px from the top) and the bottom edge is cut off near the bottom nav area, leaving minimal breathing room.
findings.impact: The tight vertical fit may cause the bottom buttons ('Recruiter', 'Other') to feel cramped or be partially obscured by system UI (home indicator on iOS), increasing mis-tap risk.
findings.fix: Add symmetric vertical margin (at minimum 24px top and bottom) to the modal, or scroll-enable the modal content on very small screens to ensure all options are comfortably reachable.

strengths: The two-column grid layout for role buttons is clean, scannable, and scales consistently from desktop to mobile without reflow issues.
The modal's focused single-question design (one decision, no extraneous UI) is appropriately minimal for a first-run onboarding moment.
pages.summary: The role-selection onboarding modal has a coherent minimal structure but suffers from a missing confirmation affordance, ambiguous role abbreviations, a disorienting mismatch with live breadcrumb state, and contrast/accessibility concerns that collectively risk confusing or excluding users at the critical first interaction with Forge.

findings.observation: Each skill card has both a 'Copy' button and a 'Download .md' button, but the 'Copy' button sits inside the code snippet area with no label clarifying what it copies (the curl install command, the snippet content, or the skill itself).
findings.impact: Users cannot confidently predict what will be in their clipboard after clicking Copy, leading to trial-and-error usage.
findings.fix: Label the button more explicitly, e.g. 'Copy curl' or 'Copy install command', or add a tooltip on hover that names what will be copied.

findings.observation: The '+ Subscribe' button and the author handle (e.g. '@anthropics') sit at the same visual weight as the primary 'Download .md' CTA in the card footer, creating three competing actions at the same level.
findings.impact: Users must parse three items of equal prominence to find the primary action, slowing decision-making on every card.
findings.fix: Reduce the visual weight of the author handle (plain text, no button chrome) and make '+ Subscribe' a ghost/outline style so 'Download .md' clearly reads as the primary action.

findings.observation: The code snippet preview inside each card is truncated with ellipsis and shows raw SKILL.md frontmatter syntax (e.g. '--- name: webapp-testing description: Text web applications...'), which is low-signal noise for a browsing user.
findings.impact: The preview space is consumed by metadata syntax rather than useful signal about what the skill actually does, making card scanning less efficient.
findings.fix: Replace the raw frontmatter preview with a rendered one-liner summary pulled from the description field, or show the first meaningful line of the skill's prompt body.

findings.observation: There is a lone '?' icon at the far right of the global nav bar with no label or tooltip visible in the screenshot.
findings.impact: Users cannot determine whether this is help, a keyboard shortcut reference, or account info without clicking it, adding cognitive overhead.
findings.fix: Add a visible label 'Help' next to the icon, or at minimum expose a tooltip on hover that names the destination.

findings.observation: Category labels on cards (e.g. 'TESTING', 'DEVELOPMENT', 'DOCUMENTS') are rendered in all-caps with very small type inside a subtle pill, making them hard to read against the dark card background.
findings.impact: Users who want to visually scan by category across the card grid must work harder than necessary to read the labels.
findings.fix: Increase the category label font size by 1–2px and raise the contrast of the pill background or text color to meet WCAG AA on dark surfaces.

findings.observation: On mobile the filter category pills ('Development', 'Testing', 'Debugging', 'Planning', 'Code Review', 'Documents', 'Other') appear to be clipped or require horizontal scrolling with no visual affordance (no fade edge or scroll indicator) indicating there are more options.
findings.impact: Mobile users may not discover all filter categories, causing them to miss relevant filtering options.
findings.fix: Add a right-side fade gradient on the pill row to signal horizontal scrollability, or collapse filters into a dropdown/sheet on mobile.

findings.observation: Vote counts are shown as '↑ 2', '↑ 1', or '↑ 0' but most cards show '0' with no visual differentiation. The upvote control lacks any active/toggled state, so users cannot tell if they have already upvoted a skill.
findings.impact: Users may upvote multiple times unknowingly or be unsure if their vote registered, reducing trust in the voting system.
findings.fix: Add a visually distinct toggled state (e.g. filled arrow icon, accent color) to the upvote control when a user has already voted.

strengths: The three-column card grid on desktop makes efficient use of the 1440px canvas, keeping a large number of skills scannable without excessive scrolling.
The '- Install with curl' command embedded directly in each card is a strong developer-experience touch that eliminates the need to navigate to a separate documentation page.
pages.summary: The Skills Library page has a solid information architecture and good developer-focused affordances, but suffers from ambiguous button labeling, competing call-to-action hierarchy within cards, and low-signal code previews that collectively slow card scanning and action confidence.

findings.observation: The 'Installed 9' badge shows count 9 but only 3 cards are visible. No explanation is given for the missing 6 items, no pagination, no load-more, no scroll indicator.
findings.impact: Users will believe items are missing or the interface is broken, eroding trust in the platform.
findings.fix: Either render all 9 items (with infinite scroll or pagination) or correct the badge count to match visible items. If items are filtered or loading, show explicit state.

findings.observation: The page is described as 'my_tools — User's own submissions across statuses' but the tab structure shows 'Installed', 'Saved for later', and 'Skills' — language that reflects a consumer/library view, not a publisher/submission view. There is no 'My Submissions', 'Drafts', 'Published', or 'Under Review' tab.
findings.impact: A user trying to manage their own published or pending tools has no clear path to do so, creating confusion between the consumer and creator contexts.
findings.fix: Introduce a dedicated 'My Submissions' or 'Published' tab with appropriate status indicators (draft, under review, live). Separate consumer and creator contexts clearly.

findings.observation: The running Pluely card uses a green 'Focus' button as its primary action, while the non-running cards use a blue 'Launch' button. The color and label both change simultaneously with no legend or tooltip explaining what 'Focus' means versus 'Launch'.
findings.impact: Users unfamiliar with the distinction between Focus and Launch will be uncertain what action they are taking, potentially disrupting a running session.
findings.fix: Document the state difference with a visible label or tooltip on the status indicator. Consider keeping the button label consistent (e.g., 'Open') and using color alone to reflect state, paired with an accessible aria-label.

findings.observation: The 'Remove' button sits immediately adjacent to 'Launch'/'Focus' with no visual separation (no spacing group, no divider, no destructive styling). Both buttons share similar size and weight.
findings.impact: Users risk accidentally removing an app when intending to launch or focus it, especially on mobile where tap targets are close together.
findings.fix: Style 'Remove' as a destructive secondary action: use a muted color (e.g., red-tinted or ghost with red text), increase horizontal gap from the primary CTA, and add a confirmation dialog on click.

findings.observation: '0 opens · external' is shown on every card but provides no contextual explanation of what 'external' means or why opens are always 0.
findings.impact: The metadata row occupies space but delivers no actionable value to the user, adding visual noise without insight.
findings.fix: Either explain 'external' with a tooltip (e.g., 'Opens in an external window') and only show open counts once non-zero, or remove the row until meaningful data exists.

findings.observation: Status indicators ('Not running', 'Running · 48m') rely solely on a small colored dot with no text differentiation beyond label color for the running state. The dot is approximately 8px and has no accessible label.
findings.impact: Users with color vision deficiency or using screen readers cannot reliably distinguish running from not-running states.
findings.fix: Add aria-label to the status dot (e.g., aria-label='Status: Running'), use a shape or icon difference in addition to color, and ensure the 'Running' text itself is always present.

findings.observation: Card descriptions are truncated inconsistently: 'OpenSuperWhisper' wraps to 3 lines, 'Raycast' to 2 lines, and 'Pluely' to 3 lines within fixed-height cards, causing uneven visual rhythm across the grid.
findings.impact: The grid feels unpolished and makes systematic comparison between tools harder.
findings.fix: Clamp all descriptions to 2 lines with CSS line-clamp and a consistent card min-height so all cards align at the same baseline.

findings.observation: On mobile the top navigation wraps awkwardly: 'anonymous' and 'set email' appear on a separate line above 'Apps Skills My Forge + Publish', creating a two-row nav that consumes ~60px of vertical space.
findings.impact: Reduces visible content area and looks unintentional, undermining the professional feel of the product.
findings.fix: Collapse the account controls into a hamburger or avatar icon on mobile to keep the nav single-row, or move 'anonymous / set email' to a profile drawer.

findings.observation: The tab label 'Saved for later' with count 0 and 'Skills' with count 0 are both always visible, occupying tab bar space for empty states the user has not engaged with.
findings.impact: Tabs with zero items create false affordance and make the interface feel emptier than it is.
findings.fix: Either hide zero-count tabs until content exists, or show a brief inline empty state message when a zero-count tab is selected rather than surfacing empty tabs permanently.

strengths: The running-state distinction for Pluely (green dot, uptime counter '48m', Focus button) communicates live status at a glance in a way that is immediately scannable.
Card layout is clean and consistent on both viewports with clear app identity (icon, name, description) that scales well from desktop grid to mobile list.
pages.summary: The My Forge page has a solid card foundation but suffers from a critical count mismatch (badge says 9, only 3 shown), absent creator/submission workflows for a page described as 'own submissions', and several affordance and accessibility gaps around destructive actions and status indicators.

findings.observation: The Admin key input field is a plain text field with no visible password masking (type='password'). Based on the placeholder text 'Admin key' and context, if this is rendering as type='text', the key would be visible on screen.
findings.impact: Anyone viewing the screen over the user's shoulder or in a screen recording would see the admin key in plaintext, compromising security.
findings.fix: Ensure the input is type='password' with an optional show/hide toggle icon inside the field.

findings.observation: There is no error state visible and no indication of what happens if the wrong admin key is entered — no helper text, no retry limit warning, no feedback mechanism shown.
findings.impact: Users who enter an incorrect key have no idea whether they will be locked out, how many attempts they have, or what to do if they have lost the key.
findings.fix: Add a short helper line below the input such as 'Incorrect key? Contact your system administrator.' and implement visible inline error feedback on failed submission.

findings.observation: The instruction reads 'Enter the admin key (from ADMIN_KEY env var).' The parenthetical 'from ADMIN_KEY env var' is developer-facing jargon exposed directly to the end user.
findings.impact: Non-developer admins or operators won't know what an env var is, creating confusion about where to find the key.
findings.fix: Replace with plain language: 'Enter the admin key provided by your system administrator.' Keep the env var reference only in developer documentation.

findings.observation: The header shows navigation links — Apps, Skills, My Forge — on the admin gate page, which is a pre-authenticated state. These links may lead to protected routes or expose navigation before the user has proven identity.
findings.impact: Clicking any nav link before authentication could bypass the gate or create a confusing partial-access experience.
findings.fix: Hide or disable the nav links until admin access is confirmed, or clearly scope them as public-facing links separate from admin content.

findings.observation: The card containing the admin gate is vertically centered near the upper third of the viewport, leaving a large expanse of empty black space below on both desktop and mobile.
findings.impact: The page feels unfinished and the visual weight distribution is awkward, particularly on desktop where the card is small relative to the canvas.
findings.fix: True vertical center the card (50% viewport height) or add subtle brand context below the card — e.g. a brief line about Forge — to fill the space meaningfully.

findings.observation: On mobile the nav items 'Apps', 'Skills', 'My Forge' are inline in the header alongside the logo, leaving very little horizontal breathing room at 375px.
findings.impact: On smaller devices or with longer localized strings, the nav items could overlap with the logo, causing a broken layout.
findings.fix: Collapse navigation into a hamburger menu or omit it entirely on the admin gate page for mobile viewports.

findings.observation: The 'Continue' button has no loading or disabled state shown, and there is no indication of what 'Continue' leads to after successful authentication.
findings.impact: On slow connections a user may click multiple times, and there is no sense of progress or outcome expectation.
findings.fix: Add a loading spinner state to the button on submission and disable it after first click to prevent double submission.

strengths: The centered card with clear 'Admin access' heading and single-action form keeps cognitive load minimal — the user immediately knows what is required.
The dark theme is consistent between desktop and mobile with no jarring color or contrast breaks in the core UI elements.
pages.summary: The admin gate page is structurally clean but has meaningful security, copy clarity, and navigation scoping issues that could confuse non-technical users or expose the admin key entry flow to risk.
